summary: Stancil argues that the usual business “physics” used to critique AI startups—burn rates, margins, subsidies—don’t fit a market moving at unprecedented speed and scale. When demand is exploding and prices are still being discovered, the classic equations feel outdated, even if new constraints will eventually reassert themselves. He then pivots to the ethics of AI companionship, suggesting our moral instincts may be “dumbfounded” rather than well-reasoned, while companies rush toward engagement-maximizing chatbot products anyway.
money_quotes: Which is to say—on this extreme edge of economic physics, it seems very unlikely that the normal laws of business apply in the way that we’re used to.
Nobody knows what a token should be worth.
Would it matter if everyone was constantly talking about burritos? Would it matter if every Fortune 500 CEO was issuing threatening memos about the existential importance of becoming burrito-enabled?
Why engage with the complex ethical questions of computational near-sentience when you can just hook up with it instead?
key_insight: In a market as fast and pervasive as AI, traditional business and moral frameworks lag reality, yet companies keep racing ahead regardless.
